title: Chapter Twenty-Five: Round Eight
content: Round Eight.
Not in Jessel's journal. Not in Peter's files. Not in any Design document, any protocol sheet, any contingency plan that Miles Ashford has memorized down to the punctuation.
A round that was never designed.
It's emerging organically — a mutation, a collision between the Game and the internet, between control and chaos. Whatever happens next, Peter didn't plan it.
No one did.

I set up the stream at 11:47 PM. New platform. Offshore hosting, no content moderation, no takedown requests, no corporate bureaucracy. I paid for it in crypto three days ago when Mrs. Grose was distracting Miles with questions about the east wing piping. The satellite uplink from Bly's control room feeds it. The security camera loops I've been recording for a week mean the feeds show what I want them to show — or, right now, what I *need* them to show. The truth.
I sit in front of the camera. The ring light is warm on my face. My hands are steady.
I press Go Live.
"My name is Maeve Bly."

The words come out clear. No tremor. No hesitation. Not the uncertain woman from the first Reddit post, the one who typed in lowercase and apologized for taking up space. Not the compliant patient who swallowed whatever narrative the hospital fed her. Someone new. Someone who decided.
"Peter Quint was my father. I came to Bly Manor to find out how he died."
I let that land. The chat is already moving — slow at first, then faster.
"I found something worse. He designed a game. It has consumed seven women. I have the documents. I have the recordings. I have the testimony. And I'm going to end it. Starting now."
The chat explodes.

Fifty thousand viewers in three minutes. A hundred thousand in five. The numbers climb like a countdown to detonation. Three hundred thousand in ten minutes, and they're not just watching — they're sharing, clipping, translating in real time. The Reddit thread from this morning was a fuse. This is the explosion.

I answer questions for four minutes. I hold up Peter's Design documents — redacted where I need them redacted, visible where I need them visible. I name the women. All seven. Not as victims. As people. I say their names the way you say the names of the dead when you want them to be remembered, not consumed.

The camera light is warm. The platform's chat scrolls faster than I can read — a river of reaction, speculation, support, doubt. Someone is already doxxing Bly Manor's coordinates. Someone else is archiving the stream to four different platforms simultaneously. The internet is doing what the internet does: it's eating this alive, and in doing so, it's keeping it alive.
I'm halfway through explaining the scoring system when I see it.
My phone is propped next to the camera, split screen. Left half: the stream chat. Right half: the security feeds. I've been monitoring both — one eye on the audience, one eye on the house.
Miles is moving.

He's in the east corridor. Not walking — moving with purpose. That particular economy of motion he has when he's calculated something. When he's decided. He turns left at the gallery. Left again at the staircase.
The control room.

I keep looking at the camera. I keep talking. But my pulse is hammering now, and I can feel the warmth of the ring light turning from comfort to exposure. He's going for the satellite connection. He's going to cut the feed.

The stream is hosted offshore. I designed it that way on purpose. I can't shut it down from inside Bly. But that also means I can't *protect* it from inside Bly. The satellite uplink in the control room is the only pipe out. Miles knows that. He's known it longer than I have.
He reaches the end of the corridor. His hand is on the control room door.
I have maybe ninety seconds.

The impossible choice crystallizes in my skull like ice: stay on camera, keep the audience, keep the narrative — and let Miles cut the feed. The stream goes dark. Three hundred thousand people see a black screen. There will be conspiracy theories, rescue attempts, a thousand contradictory narratives, but none of them will be *mine*. I'll have lost control of the story.
Or.

Leave the broadcast. Run for the control room. Confront Miles directly. Risk him doing something off-camera — to the equipment, to the satellite, to me. With no audience watching, no archive running, no proof of what happened.
On camera, I am untouchable. Off camera, I am just a woman in a house with a sixteen-year-old who has already proven he will do whatever the Game requires.
But if I stay on camera, the feed dies anyway.
There is no good choice. There is only the less catastrophic one.
I look directly into the lens.
"I have to go."
The chat spikes.
"Keep watching the security feeds. They're running on a separate loop — if this stream goes dark, the cameras will still be recording locally. If the feed cuts out, something has happened to me."
I stand. The ring light catches the edge of my jaw. I must look like a ghost — backlit, half-formed, already leaving.
"Don't let them make this disappear."

I kill the on-camera segment. The stream continues — it now shows the security feeds I've routed to it, the empty chair, the blank wall of my room. But the audio is still live. The audience can still hear. It's not much, but it's something.
I run.

The corridor is cold. The stones beneath my feet are like ice through thin shoes — I didn't plan for running, I planned for broadcasting, and now I'm paying for it. The manor is dark. The security lights cast everything in that blue-grey wash that makes Bly look like a crime scene photo of itself. Which, I suppose, it is.

My breath is loud. My footsteps are louder. I pass the gallery and I catch my own reflection in the dark windows — pale, running, determined. A woman chasing a sixteen-year-old boy through a dead man's house at midnight. The absurdity of it. The necessity.
Left at the staircase. Left again.
The control room door is open.

Miles is standing at the breaker panel. His hand rests on the satellite cutoff — a heavy red switch, industrial-grade, the kind that stays in the down position once you throw it. His back is to me. He doesn't turn when I enter. He heard me coming. Of course he did. He's been hearing me coming since I left my room.
"Don't."

One word. It fills the small room. The control panels blink around him — green lights, amber lights, the red of the satellite uplink holding steady for now. The room is warm with electronics, a different temperature entirely from the corridors. It smells of ozone and dust.
Miles turns. Slowly. His face is composed. His eyes are flat. He looks exactly like someone who has been playing a game his whole life and has just encountered an unexpected move.
"Why shouldn't I?"
His voice is steady. Curious, even. Not angry. Not afraid. He's genuinely asking. He wants me to convince him. He wants to see if I can.
"Because if you cut the feed, they'll know."
He doesn't flinch. But his fingers tighten on the switch. Not pulling it yet. Holding it. The way you hold something you're not ready to let go of.
"They'll know *what*?"
"That you have something to hide."

A beat. The servers hum. Somewhere, three hundred thousand people are watching a security camera feed of an empty chair and listening to audio they can't quite parse. Or maybe they can. Maybe they're leaning into their screens, ears straining, and they can hear us — distorted, echoey, but audible. I'm counting on that.
"The feed is your alibi too, Miles."
He goes very still. The first crack in the composure. Not a break — a crack. Hairline. You'd miss it if you weren't looking.

"Without it," I say, "you have no proof of what happens next. If this stream goes dark and I turn up hurt — or worse — the internet will assume you did it. They have your name. They have your face. They have Bly's coordinates, and you better believe someone's already called the police. If the feed dies right now, every single person watching will conclude that you killed it to hurt me in private."

His jaw tightens. He's running the calculation. I can see it — the rapid eye movement, the slight parting of his lips as he processes. He's fast. He's always been fast. But this is a logic problem with no winning move, and he knows it.

"They'll assume the worst," I continue. "About both of us. You won't be a witness, Miles. You'll be a suspect. And so will I. Without the feed, we're just two people in a house where seven women disappeared. Who's going to believe either of us then?"

His hand is still on the switch. But his grip has changed. It's no longer the grip of someone about to throw it. It's the grip of someone who needs to touch it to feel in control — and is starting to realize that control was always an illusion.

"If the feed stays up," I say, "the world watches. They watch you. They watch me. If something happens to me on camera, every single person knows who did it. If something happens to you on camera, every single person knows who did it. The feed doesn't protect me more than you. It protects us *equally*."
He lets go of the switch.
It's not a dramatic release. His fingers uncurl. His hand drops to his side. He exhales — a short, sharp breath through his nose, the sound of someone who has been outmaneuvered and knows it.
"The feed protects the truth," I say. "Whatever that truth is."

He looks at me. And for the first time in thirty-eight days — for the first time since I arrived at Bly and found a boy in a dead man's house playing a dead man's game — I see something in his eyes that isn't calculation. Isn't strategy. Isn't the flat, affectless performance of someone who learned to perform before he learned to be.
It's fear.
He's sixteen years old. He's in over his head. And he just realized that the ocean was always deeper than he thought.
"Okay," he says. Quietly. "Okay."

He steps back from the breaker panel. The red switch stays up. The satellite light holds steady. Somewhere offshore, a server keeps streaming. Somewhere in the world, three hundred thousand people are still watching.
I breathe.

The room is silent except for the hum of electronics and the rain against the small window above the panel. November rain. It comes in gusts, rattling the glass, and the wind makes the old manor groan like something alive and in pain.
"We should—"
A sound from below.
Glass breaking.

Not small glass. Not a window cracked by wind or a picture frame tipping off a wall. The specific, unmistakable percussion of a large pane shattering — the kind of sound that a front door makes when someone puts an elbow through it.
We both freeze.

Miles looks at me. I look at him. For one suspended second, we are not adversaries. We are two people in a house that has just been breached, and we are both thinking the same thing: *that wasn't supposed to happen.*
I grab my phone.
The security feeds. The front entrance. Camera 4.
The front door is open.

Not unlocked. Not ajar. *Open.* Swung wide on its hinges, the wind tearing through the doorway, rain blowing in at an angle, catching the foyer light and scattering it into silver threads. The marble floor is wet. Footprints — two sets — dark against the pale stone. Fresh. Recent. Real.
And then, entering the frame: two figures.

I can't make out faces. The rain blurs the camera. The foyer light flickers. One figure is tall, broad-shouldered. The other is shorter, moving quickly. They're not running. They're not hesitant. They're walking into Bly Manor with the confidence of people who have decided to be here.

The chat is going insane. Three hundred thousand people just watched two strangers walk into the house. The comments are a wall of capital letters and exclamation marks. Someone is calling the police. Someone else is saying the police are already here. Someone is posting the number for a crisis hotline.
I look at Miles.

His face is blank. Not composed — blank. The expression of someone whose entire framework has just been invalidated. The Game was closed. The Game was contained. The Game was *his.* And now the real world has walked through the front door.
"This wasn't—" he starts.
"I know."

The figures move deeper into the foyer. One of them looks up at the camera. The light catches their face. I can't read it. The feed is too grainy, the angle too oblique. But they're looking at the camera. They know it's there. They know they're being watched.
Three hundred thousand people are watching them.
Three hundred thousand people are watching us.
The rain hammers the control room window. The servers hum. The satellite light holds.

And somewhere below us, in the house that Peter built and we unmade, two strangers are standing on the marble floor, and nothing — not the Game, not the Design, not any plan any of us ever made — prepared us for what comes next.
Round Eight.
It found us.
